Jon: 	We’re going to take questions and answers at this time, from our
friends, Joshua. We can start the Q&A at this time. Let’s get some
questions.
Josh: 	What is a good link for the entire close? This is from Heather. Is it 10
to 15 minutes. I know there’s probably no exact link. What would be
your advice for that?
Jon: 	There’s no links. I don’t think in terms of links, Heather, and I don’t
think you should either.
I think what you want to think in terms of is those parts. In other words,
there’s certain parts in there that you may have 10 slides, where I may
have 2. But there’s not going to be a part in there where you have 100
slides and I have one.
So, the close, if I was to read that entire close off to you. Like literally,
if I was to read the grand offer from start to finish, it would probably be
about 10 minutes, if that, because it’s going to go by faster than it
looks, obviously, as we’re dissecting it step by step.
So, don’t think of limiting yourself to a time. Think of getting the stuff in
there. And remember that video sales letters are very successful when
they’re 35 minutes long. It works. Hopefully, that helps.
Josh: 	Next, this is from Penny. When you’re going over the one-time
payment of $47, is that the same place that you put their multiple
payments? Do you put 3 payments of whatever in the same place?
Or is there a change if you have more than one payment?
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 1 of 16 --

2
Jon: 	Nope, that’s exactly where you put it, Penny. Great thing to bring up.
Thank you for doing that, because I totally forgot to tell people about
that.
Yes, this si where you’d say, “Only one payment of $47, or…” And let
me give you a great tip here, Penny. What I say is, “Or, you can pay
slightly more and get it in 2 payments of $31.”
In other words, when you do 2 payments, most of the time, you charge
some sort of interest on that. If it was $47, my 2 payments might be
$27, 2 payments of $27.
So, I’ll say, “Or, you can pay a little more.” Because what’s their mind
going to hear? “Pay a little more.” I want people to take the one-pay
option. And the vast majority of the time, they take the one-pay option
when you phrase it that way. But that’s exactly where you put it.
Josh: 	Okay, great. This is from Louie. This is a fantastic question. If your
3X triple guarantee, what if it’s a brick-and-mortar or something, where
they’ve already consumed a physical product or, in his case, a bed and
breakfast. They’ve already stayed. What elements can he still use?
Obviously, it’s a very, very low refund rate, but just in case, how would

Jon: 	Louie, do you offer a guarantee now? Is there any sort of guarantee
with your business?
Josh: 	He’s kind of talking as if he’s doing this already.
Jon: 	Why don’t we get that question first? Again, for bed and breakfasts,

a guarantee, you just have to skip the guarantee section.
Josh: 	He said no, they don’t have one.
Jon: 	Okay, then I’d skip the guarantee. If you put something like, “We are

here,” what you could do is change this to what’s called a pledge.
Let’s say you give the price and you say, “And let me give you our total
commitment pledge. We are totally committed to your 100%
satisfaction in your stay with us. If, for any reason, during your stay
with us, you require something in addition or you have any feedback
fro us, you have my immediate attention, or you go to blank,” or
whatever.
So, that is almost like a guarantee. That’s a good place to put it.

Josh: 	Okay, this is from David. This is a great question. He’s selling a
product that is $997. That’s a high price point. He individually sells,
when you add up all of the bonuses, for $2,250.
He said, “Usually, what happens is people want to think about this
purchase and then come back. But since you take them off the page,
they won’t be able to come back. You say, “It’s only available for this
time.”
He’s wondering if you say that, will it close on the possibility of them
coming back to buy later?
Jon: 	If somebody wants your product, they’re going to come back to buy it
later. What I’ve gotten, many times, is someone emails me and goes,
“Look, I closed out, I had to talk it with my wife. Can I buy your
product?” Then, you just send them the link to buy.
What I do is, when they close out, they don’t get the super-bonus. The
super-bonus is fast action-takers. But I don’t say, in the video sales
letter, “You have to make the decision before the presentation ends, to
buy the product.” I say, “If you decide before the presentation ends,
you get the super-bonus.” Big distinction there. Big distinction.
Josh: 	Okay. So, Penny asks another good question. Would you ever have 2

But, the code that we’ve given you will work to put 50 buttons

So, in other words, let them make that decision on the next page.

Josh: 	Okay, this is a great question from Richard. He’s asking, “Once they

Jon: 	Ah, Richard, guess what? I answered that question in the tutorial
videos. You use the target=blank.
This is especially true in webinars. But use target=blank, which means
it will open in a new tab, in a new window.
That being said, I am a huge believer in testing, and you do need to
have, I believe Google Website Optimizer will do this, it’s free, you get
a 50/50 split-test going. So, you’ll test one video where it stops, and
goes right to the order page, and another where it doesn’t.
But let me tell you my preference and why I think it’s better to keep
going.
You will notice, and you probably have noticed from the copy that I’ve
written for the sample – which, by the way, I use my own formula all
the time now – I used this formula to write as I was creating the
tutorials.

more – I’m actually going to use this copy. I’m actually going to test it,
because it’s pretty damned good.
So, I’m thinking, “Wow, man, this is cool! The formula works!”
But yeah, I use it all the time. But sometimes, I don’t use it exactly
step-by-step, because I know it. So, just FYI.

-- 5 of 16 --

6
So, what we just talked about is shopping cart abandonment rate. The
next best thing to having a video on the page, which is what I said
would be the best thing to do, because you could say, “Hey, thanks a
lot for taking action. Follow-through, just click this button. Here’s what
you’re going to see,” the next best thing is having you talk, especially if
they click right before the “here’s what to expect” kind of thing, or if
they click during the questions and answers, and they’re on the page
checking out. And you go, “You know, a lot of people ask blank, and
here’s the answer to that question.”
You’re just reinforcing their decision to buy. You’ll notice that I’ve
taken that into consideration. All these slides and all these steps are
extremely specifically designed, I’ve hinted at the sticky parts. Didn’t
want to confuse a lot of people by saying, “Oh, you have to make this
part sticky.” I just did it for you. But I pointed it out in this video.
I know, from doing this for years, where people are going to exit and
where they’re not. No one in their right mind’s going to exit when I say,
“I’ve got a great deal for you. Check this out.”
That’s why we want to take advantage of that. I know, it’s sly, it’s
borderline sinister, but it’s ninja. And that’s what you’re paying this for.
You’re paying to get ninja stuff, man. And it’s kicking.
So, when you’re on that checkout page and they’re still hearing your
voice, going, “And, let me tell you what else you’re going to get,”
they’re going to be going, “Oh, I’m just going to keep on typing.” That’s
why I like it better on target=blank.
Josh: 	Next is Anthony. He says he’s working with a personal injury attorney
site, and it’s all __ (12:25) basis, of course. So, how do you handle the
price section of this, or does he leave it out altogether?
Jon: 	Thanks for asking, because it’s rare that you do something that’s on a
pro bono. Not pro bono, but it’s on a… What’s the legal word for it?
Josh: 	Contingency.
Jon: 	Contingency. Thank you very much. It’s so funny, I’m totally focused
during my own presentation, and afterwards I’m like mud. My brain
has turned into some kind of gelatinous substance or something.
So, if you’re on contingency, this is wide open. It’s like, “Let me tell
you what this is going to cost you today: nothing. We don’t make a
penny unless you make a penny. Absolutely nothing.”
Hopefully this is true. “Here’s how much our firm charges per hour.”
Or, if you don’t and never have, then you could say, “Here’s how much
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

7
the average attorney charges per hour. And then if you want t really
good one, you’re looking at $600 an hour. And you know how many
hours it’s going to take to win a case, usually? About 100,” or
whatever it may be. “So, you’re looking at $60,000.”
“So, you’re probably wondering how much this is going to cost you.
Well, it’s not going to cost you $60,000. It’s not going to cost you
$20,000. It’s not going to cost you $1,000. What are we nuts? No, it’s
not going to cost you anything, unless we win. If we win, we take blank
part of the fee. If we lose, you pay nothing.”
I don’t know about other people, but as far as I’m concerned, when
attorneys do that, I’m all over that. I think that’s the best way to handle
that triple drop there. Compare it against what normal attorneys
charge.
Josh: 	Okay. Next, we have a question from Adam. “Do you ever offer forced
continuity in the same fashion that you do your normal prices? Such
as “get this thing, get this product today, for just this price, and you’ll
automatically be enrolled in our exclusive membership,” whatever that
is, “and you can cancel anytime.”
He wants to know what you think.
Jon: 	Let me dive into this, because this is very important for everybody to
keep a head’s-up on.
My attorneys are the former lead attorneys for the FTC. Obviously, it’s
an attorney firm. But the firm principals are the former head attorneys
for the FTC, so they kind of know their stuff.
So, when this FTC stuff came out, they were speaking at a lot of
events, and that’s where I met them. So, I’ve gotten to ask them,
obviously, a ton of questions.
Forced continuity is really going to get a lot of attention in 2012, and it’s
not in the way that’s good.
Visa has already renigged on their forced continuity. In other words,
they won’t do it. If you get caught doing it with Visa, they will literally
ban you from the merchant.
So, here’s a workaround solution to that. You can say, “Join today,
and we will enroll you, free of charge, into our 3-month free trial of
blank.” You have to say, “If you want to continue on, it’s only a small
monthly fee. If you don’t, then just consider it a 3-month gift.”
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 7 of 16 --

8
Then, when they continue on, here’s the trick: sign them up for a year.
See, this is Visa’s problem. I’m not an attorney, so you’ve got to run
this by an attorney. But this is what my attorneys say. But that doesn’t
mean anything. You need to run it by your attorney.
In the wording, and this is true for ClickBank too, by the way, in the
wording of any continuity program, the wording states that an ad
infinitum, in perpetuity kind of thing – in other words, I’m going to
charge you $9.95 a month until you have to take action, you have to
pick up the phone and call me – you can be billed for 10 years. This is
what Visa’s cracking down on, and for good reason.
So, the trick around this is you look at your stick rate, and I have never,
ever met anyone over a 9-month stick rate. I don’t care how good the
product is. There may be somebody out there, I just haven’t met them
yet.
So, if you say, “We’re going to enroll you in our one-year, fill-in-the-
blank program, and we’re going to give you the first 3 months totally
free of charge, just so you can experience it,” we’re going to give you
the first 3 months of our year-long” – that’s another way to say it –
“blank plan.” Or 30 days, or whatever.
And then at that point, they’re auto-billed, and you make that very clear
in the video sales letter, very clear on the terms of the page where they
check out, at least. If it’s not in the video sales letter, you may get in
trouble. But if it’s not in the terms of the check-out page, you will totally
get in trouble.
At that point, once they agree to it, preferably, they have to check a
box to get it. Then, there’s no way around it. In other words, they’ve
got no defense if the box is unchecked. Usually, they keep the boxes
checked, because it does increase sales.
But, again, it’s going to get you in trouble.
Then, they get the 3-month bonus, or 2-month, or 1-month. And at the
end of that, the billing cycle starts. But it’s designated as only one year
long.
The average stick rate is 2.6 months, by the way, across the board, of
all products.
So, another way of getting around this. This is all covered in 3X
Conversion Formula, by the way. David Bass and I have put together
the 3X Conversion Formula, which is a different product. But I’m giving
you guys a little bit of bonus stuff here. But if you need to have more
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 8 of 16 --

9
about that, just go to 3XConversionFormula.com, because this is huge
stuff.
Another way around this is, when it comes time for them to be billed, to
give them an option of saying, “Would you rather pay $29.95 a month
for the year’s membership, or we’ll give you the entire year for half-
off?”
So, in essence, you’ve just said, “I know my stick rate is 6 months, but
I’m going to get it all upfront.” It’s positioned as a fantastic offer for
your user, and if your stick rate’s 3 months, you’ve just doubled your
profits.
These are the tricks that we use. When I say tricks, by the way, I don’t
mean I’m tricking the client. I’m actually going above-board, to be way
above the FTC, way above Visa. In other words, way above what they
want.
By the way, in the nutritional world, where there’s supplements
involved, this is how all this came down. It all came down because of
bogus supplements, where somebody’s charged $39.95 until they die.
Anyway, we’re playing way above their board, and it really helps
everyone. It helps everyone out. So, let me know if that helped.

By the way, there’s not a wrong answer here. Just let me know yes or

Jon: 	Okay, great. To the 10%, you’re starting off on the best foot. To the

nowhere near as clever. I have a lot of faith in my programmers, and
this one guy in particular knocked it out of the park with this one.
So, whenever they hit the page again, it just goes away. In fact, I
didn’t give you the code for this because this is site-specific code, so
we couldn’t do it. But if you go to some of my other websites, if you
leave the page and come back, the video starts where you left off.
If you pause the video to read an article, and in the article it says,
“Continue your presentation,” you click that, it starts where you left off.
And the magic buy button does not come down until I tell it to in the
video.
So, I’ve seen all the cheesy variations on this, where I’ll pause the
video and I’ll go back for a few minutes, I’ll come back, and there’s a
buy button right underneath it. I’ll pause it at the one second mark.
Well, that’s just totally stupid. Honestly, that’s just ridiculous, unless
they have make more money having the buy button under the video
the whole time; which, again, that’s rare.
What you want to have is you want to have it timed. If it comes down
at 15 minutes, you don’t want it to come down any sooner or any later.
So, we have it timed to do that, already in the code. And if they come
back to the page, it will simply start over again. So, the buy button will
be gone.
Josh: 	Next is from Richard. He’s wondering if you have 3 different prices,
say $77 for this, $297 for this plus this, or $597 for all 3 of these things,
how do you structure the buy pricing in the buttons, if you have 3
different packages?
Jon: 	It’s similar to the question that was asked earlier. What I suggest is
one button, and let me tell you why I suggest one button.
You can say, “Customize my order here.” You would say, “Click the
‘Customize My Order’ to make your selection right now. So, it’s one
button for them to click.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 10 of 16 --

11
If you have 3 buttons, you have 3 times the confusion; 2 buttons, twice
the confusion.
You can have 2 buttons, but I prefer that one button, “Add to cart,
make your decision on the next page.” They go to the next page, and
it’s the checkout page. And they just go, “Do I want this one, this one,
or this one?” And that’s where they make that decision.
Now, there is an argument to be said against that. And that is that you
have an interstitial page, but you have an interstitial page anyway.
You have the order page. So, if it’s on the order page, then you have
that page anyway.
If you don’t have, then you get technical. In other words, if you have
an interstitial that goes to an order page, then maybe you could have 3
buttons. But I would stick to one. I would say, “Add to cart,
customize,” something to that effect. And just make sure they know
what the button does.
Josh: 	Next is from Louie. “Is the java script triggered in your video? Your

Jon: 	Yeah. What it is, is we’ve given you FlowPlayer. That’s the player that

I’ve got an instructional video for you guys. You need a website. So,
assuming you need a website, you need an FTP program, which is
free, like Fetch. Just Google Fetch, and that’s it. Just go onto your

login and password.
If you have an FTP administrator or programmer, this will take him 30
seconds to do. This is easy. You just create a scripts folder, and you
dump FlowPlayer into your scripts folder.
You don’t have to have it in the scripts folder, but I don’t like it sitting on
the root of the drive. So, we create a scripts folder, and that’s how all
the code is given to you, with a scripts folder.
So if you don’t have one created, stick it in there. If you do, just stick it

changing specific variables, which are all covered in the tutorials. The
variables are not difficult, by the way.

Jon: 	Yeah. I think having that much information in one VSL is probably a

Say, “Part one and part 2, I’m going to cover the tip on how to do
blank,” and they have to click another button.

you, depending on your needs. If you look below the video, right now,

magic buy button, you are going to want to look at the tutorial that will

“Option one, this room. Option 2, this room. Click to buy. Click to

ask me why, it just does.

15
So, when I send out to my list or an affiliate sends out for me, or if I
purchase traffic from like MSN, that doesn’t demand that, I don’t let

The reason I mention that is because I’ve included when you pause
the video, it drops the sales pager underneath it at the pause, and then
when you resume the video it goes away.
So, you can do all kinds of cool things like that. You can pause the
video and you can say, “Hey, here’s what’s coming up. Blank, blank
and blank. Keep watching this video. Just come back to this video,
keep watching this video.
But then I’ll say, “If you leave this page, this video will restart, so
please stay on this page.” And then, you restart the video, and 5
minutes later you can have a different text segment come down. It’s
pretty cool.

Jon: 	Oh, totally. If that’s all at all possible, then yeah.
For example, let me go back to the bed and breakfast analogy here.
If you have like a basic room and a deluxe room, and they select the

That’s just an upsell. That’s just a thought. I think that’s a great idea, if

these aren’t. In other words, some of these are specific packages. In
other words, they’re just decisions. In other words, “Do I want it in red,
yellow, blue, whatever?”
So, those kind of decisions should be done on a text-based sales
page. This is why, when I started this whole video sales letter thing, I

video sales letter. So, we get all the benefits of both. You get the
